coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical detection of miRNA-319a in rice leaf responding to phytohormones treatment based on CuO-CuWO4 and rolling circle amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bingchen
Yin, Huanshun
Zhou, Yunlei
Wang, Minghui
Wang, Jun
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: MicroRNAs play a crucial role in regulating plant growth, such as involving in the pathways of phytohormons signaling, which function in broad range of important aspects in many stages of plant development and evolution. To figure out the components part of microRNA in phytohormone signaling network, a sensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor was developed to detect miRNA-319a specifically using CuO-CuWO4 as photoactive material and in situ generation electron donor technique. Taking the advantage of rolling circle amplification, nicking endonuclease triggered exponential amplification of target molecule, specific interaction between phosphate group and phos-tag and between biotin and avidin, the developed strategy can greatly amplify the detection signal only using trace amount of target miRNA-319a. The wide linear range was obtained from 1 fM to 0.1nM and the detection limit was estimated to be 0.47 fM (S/N=3). The developed method also showed good detection specificity. The effect of five phytohormones on the expression level of miRNA-319a in rice leaves was also investigated. This will be a basal principle of investigating the signaling network of phytohormones.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical biosensor
CuO-CuWO4
MicroRNA-319a
Rolling circle amplification
Phytohormones

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rice culture and total RNA extraction
Effect of phytohormones on miRNA-319a expression in rice leaf

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of PEC sensor for microRNA detection.

(A) SEM image of CuO-CuWO4. (B) XRD pattern of CuO, CuWO4, and CuO-CuWO4. (C) HR-TEM image of CuO-CuWO4. (D) PEC responses of ITO (a), CuWO4/ITO (b), CuO/ITO (c) and CuO-CuWO4/ITO in 10mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) containing 0.02M AA (detection buffer 1).

(A) PEC response of CuO-CuWO4/ITO in 10mM Tris-HCl (pH 9.8) containing 10mM AAP and 0.1mM Mg(NO3)2 (detection buffer 2) after it was treated with different electrodes. (a) Without treatment. (b) and (c) With ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au, miRNA-319a concentration was 0.1nM (b) and 1 fM (c), respectively. (d) Avidin-ALP/phos-tag-biotin/microRNA-ssDNA/AuNPs/Au (miRNA-319a concentration was 0.1nM). (B) PEC response of CuO-CuWO4/ITO in detection buffer 2 after it was treated with different electrodes. (a) AuNPs/Au was directly treated with solution-A, then with phos-tag-biotin and avidin-ALP successively. (b) ssDNA/AuNPs/Au was successively incubated with phos-tag-biotin and avidin-ALP. (c) dsDNA/AuNPs/Au was incubated with avidin-ALP. (d) Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au. Applied potential, 0V.
The influence of CuO-CuWO4 concentration (A), RCA reaction time (B), Phos-tag-biotin concentration (C) and avidin-ALP concentration (D) on PEC response. microRNA concentration was 0.1nM.

(A) PEC response of CuO-CuWO4/ITO in detection buffer 2 with different concentration of miRNA-319a. a-f: 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000, and 100000 fM. Inset, the linear relationship between photocurrent and the logarithm value of miRNA-319a concentration. (B) The histogram of the photocurrent of the developed method for different microRNAs. MicroRNA concentration was 1 pM. a-f represents miRNA-443, miRNA-435, miRNA-390, miRNA-530, miRNA-169b, miRNA-319a, respectively.
The histogram of the influence of GA, ABA, 6-BAP, IAA and IPA on the expression level of miRNA-319a in rice seedling leaves.
The comparison of the detection performances of the developed method with other reports on microRNA detection.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical detection of miRNA-319a in rice leaf responding to phytohormones treatment based on CuO-CuWO4 and rolling circle amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, 271018, Taian, Shandong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Key Laboratory of Agricultural Environment in Universities of Shandong, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Environment in Universities of Shandong

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MicroRNAs play a crucial role in regulating plant growth, such as involving in the pathways of phytohormons signaling, which function in broad range of important aspects in many stages of plant development and evolution. To figure out the components part of microRNA in phytohormone signaling network, a sensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor was developed to detect miRNA-319a specifically using CuO-CuWO4 as photoactive material and in situ generation electron donor technique. Taking the advantage of rolling circle amplification, nicking endonuclease triggered exponential amplification of target molecule, specific interaction between phosphate group and phos-tag and between biotin and avidin, the developed strategy can greatly amplify the detection signal only using trace amount of target miRNA-319a. The wide linear range was obtained from 1 fM to 0.1nM and the detection limit was estimated to be 0.47 fM (S/N=3). The developed method also showed good detection specificity. The effect of five phytohormones on the expression level of miRNA-319a in rice leaves was also investigated. This will be a basal principle of investigating the signaling network of phytohormones.

ce:section.ce:para: Phytohormones are a kind of signal molecules, which carry information, at a low concentration, for controlling plant growth and development at a subtle and sophisticated level [1]. The signal pathways of phytohormones can be effectively managed by different regulators in the process of plant growth and development. Among them, microRNAs (miRNAs) are found to be a kind of new regulator, which greatly involved into the signal transduction pathways of phytohormones [2]. In addition, miRNAs have also been certified to involve in many functional processes in plants by targeting mRNAs for cleavage or translational repression, including leaf, shoot and root development, floral development and stress responses [3]. Additionally, it is inevitable that the interaction between phytohormones mediated network and miRNAs regulated pathways during plant growth and development happens frequently. For instances, miRNA-160 can regulate expression of ARF17 (a kind of auxin response transcription factor) [4], and miRNA-159 can be induced by abscisic acid to cleave two MYB factors (MYB33 and MYB101) mRNAs during Arabidopsis seed germination [5]. However, some of the interaction mechanism with details and exact roles has not been revealed fully understood. So interconnection between miRNAs and phytohormones in plants need to be further explored. It is significant that, after grasping this essence, we can boost the productivity of some farm plants and further make contributions to agriculture around the world. For achieving it, the sensitivity and specificity of miRNAs detection is necessary.

Up to now, many techniques have been developed for miRNAs detection, including northern blotting [6], microarray [7], real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) [8], fluorescence [9], colorimetry [10], electrochemistry [11–14], photoelectrochemistry [15], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [16,17], etc. Among them, photoelectrochemcial (PEC) method attracts our attentions due to the advantages of low cost, low background, fast detection, simple instrument and high sensitivity, which enable it surpass traditional methods [18–21] In the last decade, PEC detection platform has been variously developed for different detection targets, including miRNAs. For examples, Cao et al. developed a versatile PEC biosensing platform for miRNA let-7f detection based on DNA-CdS quantum dots (QDs) sensitized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs)-COOH combined with cyclic enzymatic amplification [22]. Pang et al. proposed a PEC aptasensor for miRNA-155 detection using CH3NH3PbI3 QDs functionalized ZnO nanosheets as the light harvester [23]. Ma et al. developed a PEC method for miRNA detection based on direct exciton−plasmon interactions between CdS QDs and Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs), where AgNPs were invoked ingeniously by catalytic Ag deposition on AuNPs surface for the stimulation of high efficient damping effect toward the excitonic responses in CdS QDs [15]. All these above developed PEC strategies exhibit superior sensitivity and excellent detection performances. Nevertheless, they all required complex signal transduction construction or miRNA hybridization with probe DNA on electrode surface, which can increase the complexity of biosensor fabrication and decrease the hybridization efficiency.

In order to conquer these shortages, a simple and sensitive PEC method was developed in this work for defecting miRNA-319a and evaluating the expression level change of miRNA-319a in rice seedlings leaves with different phytohormones treatment. By taking the merits of rolling-circle amplification (RCA), miRNA-319a and miRNA-319a-specific linear padlock DNA probe was used to amplify and produce DNA with multiple repeated base sequences in aqueous solution [24]. After RCA, the produced DNA can be further digested with Nb.BsmI nicking enzyme to generate short single-strand DNA (ssDNA, its base sequence is same to miRNA-319a), where the amount of ssDNA is significantly higher than miRNA-319a in the solution [25]. Therefore, the detection sensitivity can also be greatly improved. In addition, using indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode with directly deposited with CuO-CuWO4 as detection electrode, the transduction and construction process can also be simplified.

T4 DNA ligase, Vent (exo-) DNA polymerase, Nb.BsmI were purchased from NEB (USA). Biotin functionalized Phos-tag (Phos-tag-biotin) was obtained from WAKO (Japan). Avidin-ALP was offered by Sangon (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4, ascorbic acid (AA), tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), diethy pyrocarbonate (DEPC), gibberellin (GA), abscisic acid (ABA), 6-benzylaminopurine (6-BAP), indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) and indole-3-propionic acid (IPA) were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). L-Ascorbic acid 2-phosphate trisodium salt (AAP) was purchased from Shanghai Dibo Chemical Technology Co., LTD. (China). 3-Mcaptopropionic acid was offered by Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

MicroRNA and DNA were purchased from BAKARA (Dalian, China) and Sangon (Shanghai, China), respectively. The oligonucleotides sequences were as follows. miRNA-319a, 5′-UUG GAC UGA AGG GUG CUC CC-3′, single-base mismatched microRNA, 5′-UUG GAC UGA CGG GUG CUC CC-3′, three-base mismatched microRNA, 5′-CUG GAC UGA ATG GUG CUC CA-3′, non-complementary microRNA, 5′-UAG CUU AUC AGA CUG AUG UU-3′, probe DNA, 5′-SH-(CH2)6-GCA TTC GGA ACA TGA CGT CGC GAA TT-3′, linear padlock probe, 5′-TTC AGT CCA ATA GAA TGC AAT TCG CGA CGT CAT GTT CCG AAT GCG GGA GCA CCC-3′.

The buffers used in this work are as follows. DNA immobilization buffer, 10mM Tris-HCl, 1.0mM EDTA, 1.0M NaCl, and 1.0mM TCEP (pH 7.5). 2×DNA hybridization buffer, 20mM Tris-HCl, 2.0mM EDTA, and 2.0M NaCl (pH 7.5). RNA hybridization buffer, 1×SSC, pH 7.5. 1X ThermoPol® reaction buffer for Vent (exo-) DNA polymerase, 20mM Tris-HCl, 10mM (NH4)2SO4, 10mM KCl, 2mM MgSO4, 0.1% Triton® X-100, pH 8.8. T4 DNA ligase reaction buffer, 50mM Tris-HCl, 10mM MgCl2, 1mM ATP, 10mM DTT, pH 7.5. Nb.BsmI reaction buffer, 50mM Tris-HCl, 100mM NaCl, 10mM MgCl2, 100μg/mL BSA, pH 7.9. Phos-tag-biotin reaction buffer, 10mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.0) containing 0.1M NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, 0.4mM Zn(NO3)2. Detection buffer 1, 10mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) containing 0.02M AA. Detection buffer 2, 10mM Tris-HCl (pH 9.8) containing 10mM AAP and 0.1mM Mg(NO3)2. Washing buffer, 10mM Tris-HCl, 50mM KCl, pH 7.5.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurement was carried out on a Hitachi S-4800 (Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image was obtained with a Tecnai G20 electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands).

CuO-CuWO4 composite nano-material was synthesized according to previous report with some minor modifications [26]. In brief, 35mL of 0.1M Na2WO4·2H2O aqueous solution was added dropwise into 35mL of 0.1M Cu(NO3)2·3H2O aqueous solution. Then, the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 8.5 with 0.1M NaOH aqueous solution, and the solution was magnetic stirred for 1h. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a stainless autoclave and heated at 180°C for 24h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the sediment was collected with centrifugation at 10000rpm for 30min. The obtained product was washed thoroughly with double distilled deionized water for three times. Afterwards, the product was dried at 60°C for 10h. Finally, the product was calcined at 500°C for 3h in air.

10μL of different concentration of miRNA-319a and 10μL of 10nM linear padlock probe was first mixed in 100μL centrifuge tube, and then the mixed solution was incubated at 80°C for 5min. Afterwards, 20μL of 200 unit/mL T4 DNA ligase was further introduced into the mixed solution and incubated at room temperature for 1h. Subsequently, 10μL of the above solution was added into another 100μL centrifuge tube, and then 10μL of 1X ThermoPol® reaction buffer containing 100 unit/mL Vent (exo-) DNA polymerase and 0.1μM dNTPs were added into the tube. The mixed solution was incubated at 75°C for 60min. The reaction was stopped by incubating at 80°C for 20min. Finally, 10μL Nb.BsmI reaction buffer solution (the solution contained 200 unit/mL Nb.BsmI) was mixed with the above solution and incubated at 60°C for 1h, followed by inactivation at 80°C for 10min. The obtained solution was named as solution-A, which contained the complementary DNA fragments to probe DNA.

The gold electrode (Au electrode) was firstly polished to obtain a smooth surface with 30nm Al2O3 slurry. The obtained Au electrode was rinsed with ethanol and double distilled deionized water successively for 10min, respectively. Then, the electrode was immersed in 10mL of 3mM HAuCl4 solution containing 0.1M KNO3, and AuNPs were modified on Au electrode surface by electrochemically deposition with deposition potential of −0.2V and deposition time of 200s under magnetic stirring. The obtained electrode was noted as AuNPs/Au.

For probe DNA assembly, 5μL probe immobilization buffer containing 0.5μM probe DNA was dropped on AuNPs/Au surface and incubated for 12h in a humid cell under room temperature to complete the formation of Au-S bonding between AuNPs and SH modified probe DNA. Then, the electrode was rinsed with washing buffer for three times to remove the un-specific assembled probe DNA. The obtained electrode was named as ssDNA/AuNPs/Au. Then, 5μL of 0.1M mercaptopropionic acid was dropped on the electrode surface and incubated for 30min to obtain a well-organized electrode surface. After rinsed with washing buffer for three times, the electrode was further incubated with the mixed solution of 2X DNA hybridization buffer and solution-A for 120min at 37°C under humid conditions. Finally, the electrode was rinsed with washing buffer and noted as dsDNA/AuNPs/Au.

5μL of phos-tag-biotin reaction buffer containing 1.5μM phos-tag-biotin was dropped on dsDNA/AuNPs/Au surface and incubated for 30min at room temperature in a humid cell. Then, the electrode was rinsed with washing buffer for three times. The obtained electrode was noted as Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au. After that, the electrode was further incubated with 5μL of 10mM PBS (pH 7.5) containing 50mM KCl and 1mg/mL avidin-ALP for 1h at room temperature under humid conditions. The obtained electrode was named as ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au.

3mg CuO-CuWO4 and 0.5mg poly(vinyl alcohol) was added into 1mL double distilled deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1h to obtained a homogeneous dispersion with CuO-CuWO4 concentration of 3mg/mL. The ITO electrode (1×5cm size) was first incubated with ethanol/NaOH mixed solution (v/v, 1:1) and acetone successively with the aid of ultrasonication for 30min, then the electrode was washed thoroughly with double distilled deionized water and dried at 60°C for 1h. Then 40μL of 3mg/mL CuO-CuWO4 was dropped on ITO electrode surface and dried under infrared lamp irradiation. After that, the electrode was rinsed with double distilled deionized water and dried under N2 blowing. The obtained electrode was noted as CuO-CuWO4.

PEC detection is carried out at a home-built PEC system, where a 500W Xe lamp is used as the irradiation source for producing the visible-light (Light intensity is 20mW/cm2). The PEC response was recorded at a CHI832A electrochemical workstation (CHI instruments, Austin, USA) with a three-electrode system, where The ITO or CuO-CuWO4/ITO were used as working electrode, a Pt wire was as the counter electrode and an saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was as the reference electrode.

For electrode characterization, the photocurrent was recorded in detection buffer 1 with the applied potential of 0V. For miRNA-319a detection, CuO-CuWO4/ITO was used as working electrode. Firstly, ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au was incubated in 10mL detection buffer 2 at 37°C for 30min under magnetic stirring. Then, CuO-CuWO4/ITO was immersed in detection buffer 2 and the photocurrent was recorded with the applied potential of 0V. For detection buffer 1 and detection buffer 2, they were both deaerated by nitrogen for 30min before experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rice culture and total RNA extraction
ce:section.ce:para: Mature rice seeds were used in the experiments. Seeds were soaked and embedded in gauze for about 10days with ambient conditions for germination using deionized water as water source. When these seeds germinated, they were transferred to culture dishes and incubated at room temperature. For investigating the effect of different phytohormones (GA, ABA, 6-BAP, IAA, IPA) on the expression level of miRNA-319a, 50mg/L of phytohormone aqueous solution or water (as control) were used as water source, and six groups of rice seedlings were cultured for 24h with different water source. Then, the leaves of rice seedlings were harvested for total RNA extraction using RNA extraction kit according to the manufacturer′s recommended protocol (Invitrogen, USA). RNA integrity was confirmed by agarose gel electrophoresis and total RNA content was detected by Q3000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Quawell, USA). Before detection, the total RNA was diluted to same concentration.

The detection principle of the developed method was illustrated in Scheme 1 . When target miRNA-319a and miRNA-319a-specific linear padlock probe was mixed together, they could be hybridized. In the presence of T4 DNA ligase, the linear padlock probe can be closed and form a circular template, in which miRNA-319a can be acted as a primer for initiating the RCA reaction with the catalysis effect of Vent (exo-) DNA polymerase to generate a long repeat DNA sequence in the presence of dNTP. This long repeat DNA sequence can be digested by Nb.BsmI nicking endonuclease, generating plentiful new DNA primers to trigger a new RCA reaction, consequently leading to an exponential amplification of DNA. The amplified DNA products can be further hybridized with probe DNA at the surface of ssDNA/AuNPs/Au, where the 5′-terminal of the target DNA with phosphate group was away from the electrode surface. Afterwards, the phosphate group can be recognized by phos-tag-biotin, which could make the success immobilization of phos-tag-biotin on the electrode surface. Phos-tag is a kind of specific phosphate-binding agent. In the presence of two equivalents of Zn2+ or Mn2+, phos-tag forms a specific noncovalent complex with the phosphomonoester dianion at neutral pH. Subsequently, under the specific interaction between biotin and avidin, avidin-ALP could be further captured on the electrode surface. When this electrode was immersed into detection buffer 2, AAP can be hydrolyzed to produce of electron donor of AA for PEC detection. One point must be highlighted, that the higher the amount of microRNA, the higher concentration of target DNA can be produced, which can lead to immobilize more amount of ALP on the electrode surface to catalyze AAP hydrolysis for producing more electron donor of AA. Finally, a strong PEC response can be obtained at CuO-CuWO4/ITO electrode. According to relationship between miRNA-319a concentration and PEC response, miRNA-319a concentration can be detected with high detection sensitivity.

Fig. 1 A showed the SEM image of CuO-CuWO4. It was clear that CuO-CuWO4 exhibited hollow columnar structure. Fig. 2 B showed the XRD pattern of CuO, CuWO4, CuO-CuWO4. As seen in the curve for CuO, the 2θ of 32.58, 35.72, 39.01, 48.84, and 53.58 can be attributed to the standard diffraction of (-110), (002), (111), (-202), and (020) planes (PDF # 45-0937). The synthesized CuWO4 exhibited clear diffraction peaks with triclinic crystal structure (PDF # 70–1732). The 2θ of 15.47, 19.20, 23.11, 23.75, 24.32, 25.99, 28.75, 30.21, 30.93, 31.71, 32.28, 35.74, 36.56, 38.64, 48.81 and 53.29 can be ascribed to the standard diffraction of (010), (100), (110), (0–11), (011), (-110), (-1-11), (111), (020), (-111), (1–11), (0–21), (021), (-120), (130) and (-202) planes. For CuO-CuWO4, it has the standard diffraction of both CuO and CuWO4, indicating that the prepared compound should be CuO-CuWO4. Fig. 1C showed the HR-TEM image of CuO-CuWO4. It indicated that CuO was located on the surface of CuWO4. And the (-110) and (100) facets of CuWO4 and the (-202) facet of CuO were visible. The production of CuO can be explained as the fact that the substrate containing copper was oxidized with oxygen in the calcining process with high temperature [27]. The PEC behavior of CuO-CuWO4 was investigated in detection buffer 1 (10mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) containing 0.02M AA). As can be seen in Fig. 1D, the bare ITO electrode showed no PEC response and only a straight line can be observed (curve a), indicating that the bare ITO electrode do not possess photoelectric activity. However, obvious PEC responses were obtained for CuWO4/ITO (curve b) and CuO/ITO (curve c), which demonstrated that CuOW4 and CuO nanoparticles possessed good photoelectric activity. Interestingly, the photocurrent obtained at CuO-CuWO4/ITO (curve d) was greatly higher than that obtained at CuWO4/ITO and CuO/ITO. This phenomenon might be explained as the fact that the photo-generated electrons transfer from CuO to CuWO4 and the photo-generated holes transfer from CuWO4 to CuO would incorporate each other, and this synergism between the transfer of electron and hole could greatly increase the charge separation efficiency for both CuWO4 and CuO [26]. Therefore, the photocurrent for CuO-CuWO4/ITO improved significantly. Based on it, CuO-CuWO4/ITO was selected as the working electrode in this work.

In order to investigate the detection feasibility, several experiments were performed in detection buffer 2 (10mM Tris-HCl (pH 9.8) containing 10mM AAP and 0.1mM Mg(NO3)2). In Fig. 2A, curve a was the PEC response of CuO-CuWO4/ITO in detection buffer 2. It was clear that only a very weak PEC response (39.02nA) was obtained, which can be ascribed to the absence of ALP, resulting in the lack of electron donor of AA in detection buffer 2. However, when the detection buffer was treated with ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au (miRNA-319a concentration was 0.1nM), a strong PEC response (315.19nA) was displayed at CuO-CuWO4/ITO (curve b), indicating that ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au can effectively catalyze the hydrolysis reaction of AAP to produce electron donor of AA. And in the presence of AA, the photo-generated hole of CuWO4 can be captured by the electron of AA, which blocked the recombination of hole and electron of CuWO4, and resulted in a strong photocurrent. This result also proved the successful RCA amplification, Nb.BsmI nicking and the preparation of ALP/Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au electrode. To further confirm that the developed method can be applied to detect miroRNA-319a, its concentration was changed from 0.1nM to 1 fM. As also shown in Fig. 2A, the photocurrent of CuO-CuWO4/ITO decreased from 272.64 (curve b) to 108.77 (curve c) nA. According to the variation of the photocurrent, one can concluded that this method might be applied for microRNA detection.

In order to verify the signal amplification effect using RCA strategy, another control experiment was carried out in detection buffer 2. In this experiment, probe DNA on ssDNA/AuNPs/Au was directly hybridized with 0.1nM miRNA-319a with 5′-terminal of phosphate group, then incubated successively with phos-tag-biotin and avidin-ALP (The electrode was noted as Avidin-ALP/phos-tag-biotin/microRNA-ssDNA/AuNPs/Au). The photocurrent was 61.99nA (Fig. 2A, curve d) and it was much lower than that obtained at RCA with 0.1nM miRNA-319a. This result clearly demonstrated that RCA used in this work can greatly improve the PEC response, which can result in increasing the detection sensitivity.

For further confirming the detection feasibility, several other control experiments were performed in detection buffer 2 using CuO-CuWO4/ITO as working electrode. The photocurrent was recorded and compared after detection buffer 2 was treated with different process. (1) AuNPs/Au was directly treated with solution-A, then with phos-tag-biotin and avidin-ALP successively. (2) ssDNA/AuNPs/Au was successively incubated with phos-tag-biotin and avidin-ALP. (3) dsDNA/AuNPs/Au was incubated with avidin-ALP. (4) Phos-tag-biotin/dsDNA/AuNPs/Au. The PEC responses were shown in Fig. 2B. The photocurrent values for (1), (2), (3) and (4) were 42.68, 45.97, 44.39 and 39.12nA, respectively. These values were close to that obtained at untreated detection buffer 2 (39.02nA, shown in Fig. 2A). It indicated that avidin-ALP cannot be modified on the electrode surface due to the absence of probe DNA (1), target DNA (2), phos-tag-biotin (3) and avidin-ALP (4), respectively. These results also demonstrated that the developed method can be successively applied to detect microRNA with high accuracy.

In order to achieve high detection sensitivity, several experimental parameters were optimized, such as CuO-CuWO4 concentration, RCA reaction time, Phos-tag-biotin concentration and avidin-ALP concentration.

Fig. 3 A presented the influence of CuO-CuWO4 concentration on the PEC response of CuO-CuWO4/ITO in detection buffer 1 (10mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) containing 0.02M AA). The photocurrent increased with CuO-CuWO4 concentration changing from 1 to 3mg/mL. Then, the photocurrent decreased reversely when furthering increasing CuO-CuWO4 concentration. Thus, 3mg/mL was chosen as the optimal concentration.

The amount of target DNA was mainly depended on the RAC reaction time, so it was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, with prolonging the reaction time from 5 to 60min, the photocurrent increased gradually, indicating that more target DNA was produced with increasing reaction time. However, when furthering extending reaction time, the photocurrent increased slowly, which might be caused by the approximately saturated hybridization with probe DNA. Therefore, 60min was used in this work.

Phos-tag-biotin concentration could also influence the detection sensitivity because it was used as a bridge to link electrode and single generation unit of avidin-ALP. As shown in Fig. 3C, the photocurrent increased with Phos-tag-biotin concentration changing from 0.1 to 1.5μM. Then, the photocurrent tended to achieve a plateau with further increasing Phos-tag-biotin concentration. As a bridge reagent, more Phos-tag-biotin immobilizing on electrode surface can greatly increase the amount of Avidin-ALP, which can catalyze AAP to produce more AA. As a result, the photocurrent can be significantly improved. However, when the immobilization amount of Phos-tag-biotin tended to saturated mode, the photocurrent would stable. Thus, 1.5μM was chosen as the optimal concentration.

As signal generation unit, the influence of avidin-ALP on the photocurrent was also investigated. As seen in Fig. 3D, the optimal concentration was 1mg/mL. Therefore, this concentration was used in this work.

Under the optimal experiment conditions, the detection performances of the developed method for miRNA-319a were assessed. Firstly, the relationship between miRNA-319a concentration and photocurrent was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the photocurrent increased with miRNA-319a concentration varying from 1 to 100000 fM. The linear relationship can be obtained in this range and the linear regression equation can be expressed as I (nA)=33.47logc (fM) + 107.01 (R =0.9985) and the detection limit can be evaluated to be 0.47 fM (S/N=3) (Insert of Fig. 4A). The detection performances of the developed method were compared with other reports and the results were listed in Table 1 . The linear range was wider than some of previous work and the detection limit was lower than some of previous work, indicating that the detection strategy possessed good performances for sensitive detection of miRNA-319a.

Detection specificity is a kind of crucial property for assay method, herein it was investigated. For performing it, 1 pM miRNA-319a was replaced by the same concentration of different microRNAs (miRNA-435, miRNA-390, miRNA-530, miRNA-443, miRNA-169b) in RCA process. Then the electrode fabrication process and detection process was same to that described in Section 2. The change of the photocurrent value (ΔI = I 2 − I 1) was compared, where I 2 was the photocurrent of different microRNAs, and I 1 was the photocurrent of CuO-CuWO4/ITO. As seen in Fig. 4B, ΔI for miRNA-319a was greatly higher than that for other microRNAs, indicating that the developed method possessed good detection selectivity for miRNA-319a. Reproducibility was also investigated using 0.1nM miRNA-319a for seven RCA amplifications independently. The relative standard deviation was 6.35%, indicating acceptable detection reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of phytohormones on miRNA-319a expression in rice leaf
ce:section.ce:para: In order to testify the applicability of the developed method for real sample detection, the effect of phytohormone on the expression level of miRNA-319a in rice seedlings leaves was investigated. It has been reported that microRNAs not only control patterning of plant, but also play a crucial role in phytohormone responses [2]. That is to say, microRNAs might be involved in the pathway of plant growth and development regulated by phytohormone. Therefore, it can provide new insights for mechanism of plant growth and development when the interaction between microRNAs and phytohormone. To achieve it, it is necessary to detect the effect of exogenous phytohormone on the expression level change of microRNA in plant tissue or blade. Thus the influence of five phytohormones (GA, ABA, 6-BPA, IAA and IPA) on the expression level of microRNSA-319a in seedlings leaves was investigated. The experiment process was described in Section 2.9. The concentration of the extracted total RNA from rice leaves was 38.6, 41.16, 321.76, 85.67, 299.4 and 133.47μg/mL with the treatment of GA, ABA, 6-BPA, IAA and IPA, respectively. Total RNA content was detected by Q3000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Quawell, USA). As shown in Fig. 5 , the expression level of miRNA-319a with GA treatment was higher than that in control group, the expression level with ABA and 6-BAP treatment was lower than that in control group, and the expression level with IAA and IPA treatment was close to the value of control group. These results demonstrated that GA can increase miRNA-319a expression in rice seedlings leaves, ABA and 6-BAP can decrease miRNA-319a expression, and IAA and IPA showed little effect on miRNA-319a expression. For further proving the detection accuracy, the change of miRNA-319a expression level was also detected by qRT-PCR. The error for these two methods was lower than 10%, implying that the developed method had good detection accuracy.

In summary, a simple and sensitive PEC method was developed for miRNA-319a detection using CuO-CuWO4 as photoactive material. Using RCA reaction, nicking enzyme and ALP catalytic signal amplification, the detection sensitivity was improved greatly. More importantly, the developed method avoids the complexity of photoactive material construction and miRNA hybridization on electrode surface, which can also increase the detection sensitivity and simplify the experimental operations. In addition, the proposed detection strategy can also be applied to investigate the effect of phytohormones on miRNA expression, which can provide useful information for understanding the mutual regulation of phytohormones and miRNAs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the foundation of the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province, China (No. ZR2014BQ029, ZR2017MD023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375079, 21775090), and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFD0800304).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hormonal regulation of plant growth and development
Insights into the mechanism of plant development: interactions of miRNAs pathway with phytohormone response
MicroRNAs: at the root of plant development?
MicroRNA-directed regulation of Arabidopsis AUXIN RESPONSE FACTOR17 is essential for proper development and modulates expression of early auxin response genes

Real-time polymerase chain reaction microRNA detection based on enzymatic stem-loop probes ligation
Universal fluorescence biosensor platform based on graphene quantum dots and pyrene-functionalized molecular beacons for detection of microRNAs
Ultrasensitive, colorimetric detection of microRNAs based on isothermal exponential amplification reaction-assisted gold nanoparticle amplification

Label-free, ultrasensitive and electrochemical immunosensing platform for microRNA detection using anti-DNA:RNA hybrid antibody and enzymatic signal amplification
Ultrasensitive electrochemical sensing platform for microRNA based on tungsten oxide-graphene composites coupling with catalyzed hairpin assembly target recycling and enzyme signal amplification
Recent advances in transition-metal dichalcogenides based electrochemical biosensors: a review
Invoking direct exciton-plasmon interactions by catalytic Ag deposition on au nanoparticles: photoelectrochemical bioanalysis with high efficiency
Signal-off electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on phi29 DNA polymerase mediated strand displacement amplification for microrna detection

Photoelectrochemical bioanalysis: the state of the art
A label-free photoelectrochemical cocaine aptasensor based on an electropolymerized ruthenium-intercalator complex
A carbon nanotube/quantum dot based photoelectrochemical biosensing platform for the direct detection of microRNAs
Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical aptasensing of miR-155 using efficient and stable CH3NH3PbI3 quantum dots sensitized ZnO nanosheets as light harvester
Highly sensitive determination of microRNA using target-primed and branched rolling-circle amplification
Homogeneous and label-free detection of microRNAs using bifunctional strand displacement amplification-mediated hyperbranched rolling circle amplification

Electrochemical fabrication of complex copper oxide nanoarchitectures via copper anodization in aqueous and non-aqueous electrolytes
Detection of microRNA in clinical tumor samples by isothermal enzyme-free amplification and label-free graphene oxide-based SYBR Green I fluorescence platform

Sandwich-type microRNA biosensor based on magnesium oxide nanoflower and graphene oxide-gold nanoparticles hybrids coupling with enzyme signal amplification
Photoelectrochemical immunosensor for microRNA detection based on gold nanoparticles-functionalized g-C3N4 and anti-DNA:RNA antibody
Visible-light induced photoelectrochemical biosensor for the detection of microRNA based on Bi2S3 nanorods and streptavidin on an ITO electrode
Photoelectrochemical biosensor for highly sensitive detection of microRNA based on duplex-specific nuclease-triggered signal amplification
Intercalation of quantum dots as the new signal acquisition and amplification platform for sensitive electrochemiluminescent detection of microRNA
Highly selective detection of microRNA based on distance-dependent electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between CdTe nanocrystals and Au nanoclusters

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bingchen Li is postgraduate student of College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. His current interests are electrochemical biosensor.

Huanshun Yin received doctor degree in Soil Science in College of Resources and Environment, Shandong Agricultural University in 2012. Now he is an associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. His current interest is electrochemical bioassay.

Yunlei Zhou received doctor degree in College of Life Science, Beijing Normal University in 2013. Now she is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. Her current interest is electrochemical biosensor.
Minghui Wang is postgraduate student of College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. Her current interests are electrochemical biosensor.

Jun Wang received doctor degree in Pesticide Science in College of Plant Protection, Shandong Agricultural University in 2012. Now he is an associate Professor in College of Resources and Environment, Shandong Agricultural University. His current interest is environmental chemistry and analytical chemistry.

Shiyun Ai is a professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. He received his doctor degree (analytical chemistry) from Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University in 2004. His current research interests include bioelectroanalysis and preparation of nano-functional material.
